summary: I was born in Lubbock, Texas, on March 21, 1934. Although I didn't grow up there, my family had deep ties to the area. My grandfather even donated land for Texas Tech University in Lubbock, and helped set up the town's lights. Lubbock was an important place in my family's history and education.

My early years were spent in West Texas, near Plains, where my family were sharecroppers. We lived on farms and worked hard picking cotton, even pushing trailers to the gin. Later, my family operated concrete plants across West Texas and Eastern New Mexico. West Texas was home to much of my childhood and family business.

When I was about two years old, my family moved from West Texas to New Mexico, where I lived for about 45 years. We settled in places like Lovington, and my father worked as a cement finisher there. My grandfather also had ranching ties in New Mexico, and I attended Lovington High School. New Mexico was where I grew up, went to school, and started my career.

About 15 years ago, I moved to Conroe, Texas, after spending most of my life in New Mexico. It was a significant change after decades in the Southwest. Conroe became my home later in life, marking a new chapter away from my childhood and family roots.

I lived near Plains, Texas, during my earliest years and even got my first haircut there. My family were sharecroppers in that area, and I remember helping push cotton trailers to the gin as a small child. Plains was part of my very first memories before we moved to New Mexico.

My father started his cement finishing career in Southern California before moving on to other places like Seattle and Minnesota. Though I didn't live there, it was part of his early work history. Southern California was the beginning of his long career in construction.

Seattle was one of the places where my father worked as a cement finisher before settling in Texas and New Mexico. He had a varied career across the country, and Seattle was part of that journey. Though I didn't live there, it was important in his professional life.

My father worked extensively in Minnesota, particularly around St. Paul, as a cement finisher before moving to Texas and New Mexico. His experience there was part of a long career that spanned many states. Minnesota was a significant chapter in his working life.

St. Paul, Minnesota, was a key place where my father worked for many years as a cement finisher. He gained a lot of experience there before moving to Texas and New Mexico. Although I didn't live there, it was important in shaping his career.

Lovington was my hometown in New Mexico where I attended Lovington High School and lived much of my life. My father worked there as a cement finisher, and we operated several concrete plants in the area. Lovington was central to my upbringing and family business.

During World War II, my father had to leave Lovington to work in the shipyards in Houston because he was not drafted due to his prior service in World War I. The war made life tough, with shortages and rationing affecting everyone. I remember how hard it was for my mother and me to manage on our own during those years.

My father served in World War I in Germany as an ambulance and motorcycle driver in the United States Army Air Forces. He had a very eventful time and was even considered for parachutist duty, which he declined. His military service in World War I exempted him from being drafted in World War II.

My father served in Germany during World War I as part of the United States Army Air Forces. He was an ambulance and motorcycle driver and had many eventful experiences there. His time in Germany shaped much of his military background.

My father served in the United States Army Air Forces during World War I, where he worked as an ambulance and motorcycle driver in Germany. He was even considered for parachutist training but declined. His service was a significant part of his life and exempted him from being drafted in World War II.

I attended Lovington High School in Lovington, New Mexico, where I was honored by both students and faculty for my citizenship and courtesy. I graduated with two full-page pictures in the yearbook, which was a proud moment. Lovington High was where I spent my formative school years.

Lovington, where I lived, is located between Hobbs and Clovis on the east side of New Mexico. Hobbs was a nearby town that helped define the region where my family lived and worked. It was part of the eastern New Mexico area that I knew well.

Clovis is another town near Lovington, New Mexico, where I grew up. It was part of the eastern New Mexico region that shaped my early life. Clovis, along with Hobbs, helped define the area where my family lived and worked.

My grandfather, Monster Lewis, and his wife lived in Roswell, New Mexico, where they worked for the Chisholm Cattle Company. Roswell was part of my family's ranching history and connection to cattle driving. It was an important place in my family's past.

The Chisholm Cattle Company employed my grandfather and his wife in Roswell, New Mexico. They were involved in cattle driving and ranching, with properties south of Roswell and near Tatum. The company was a key part of my family's ranching heritage.

Tatum, New Mexico, was near one of the Chisholm Cattle Company's ranches where my grandfather worked. It was northeast of Tatum that the last 3,000 buffalo in the U.S. were killed. Tatum and its surroundings were part of my family's ranching landscape.

Causey, New Mexico, is named after a man who killed the last 3,000 buffalo in the United States near Tatum. This area was part of the ranching country connected to my family and the Chisholm Cattle Company. Causey is a small town with historical significance in our region.

My father was such a skilled cement finisher that he was invited to do the steps on the Empire State Building. However, he chose to come to Texas instead. This invitation shows the high regard people had for his craftsmanship.

Adams Concrete was one of the family companies my father operated, including ready-mix plants and gravel businesses. He was actively working at the Fort Sumner plant even in his 90s. Adams Concrete was a major part of our family's business legacy.

Lovington Ready Mix was part of the family business that grew from small mixers to 14 concrete plants across West Texas and Eastern New Mexico. I worked in the business from a young age, helping my father who was a dedicated cement finisher. Lovington Ready Mix was central to our livelihood.
cues: West Texas and Eastern New Mexico

summary: Potash Sand & Gravel was another company in our family’s construction and concrete business. Alongside Adams Concrete and Lovington Ready Mix, it helped us grow a significant operation in the region. These companies were the backbone of our family’s work life.

After high school, I went to college in Pasadena, California, for a couple of years and had a wonderful time there. It was a beautiful school and a new experience for me before transferring to Texas Tech. Pasadena was my first taste of college life away from home.

I started attending Texas Tech University in 1954, encouraged by my mother and grandfather who had a strong love for the school. My grandfather had donated land for the university in Lubbock, and I studied civil engineering and business administration there. Texas Tech was where I earned my degree and prepared for my career.
cues: degree in business and civil engineering

summary: My grandfather came from Knoxville, Tennessee, and made a remarkable journey on horseback with his brother to Midland, Texas. This journey took two years and was part of the family's pioneering history. Knoxville was the starting point of that adventure.

Midland, Texas, was the destination of my grandfather and his brother after leaving Knoxville, Tennessee, on horseback. It was part of their westward migration and the beginning of our family's Texas roots. Midland played a role in our family's settlement story.

One of my grandfather's brothers went west to El Paso, Texas, to work after their journey from Tennessee. El Paso was part of the family's spread across Texas. Also, when I was drafted, I was sent to El Paso for processing.

My grandfather moved to the northern part of Lea County, New Mexico, after leaving the Chisholm Cattle Company. He became a windmill man there and used large sections of land in both New Mexico and West Texas. Lea County was an important area for our family's ranching activities.

Before World War II, the New Mexico National Guard was called up to defend the Philippines against Japanese attacks. I recall stories about the soldiers having to cross floods to get to buses and how many were captured or killed. Their sacrifice was deeply felt in our community.

The Philippines campaign was a tragic time for many New Mexico National Guard soldiers who were sent to fight the Japanese. Many were captured and endured years as prisoners of war, with few surviving. Their experiences left a lasting impact on our small towns back home.

The Bataan Death March was a horrific ordeal endured by soldiers from New Mexico and elsewhere captured by the Japanese in the Philippines. Many never returned, and those who did carried physical and emotional scars. Our town deeply mourned the loss and supported survivors as best we could.

Brownfield, Texas, was mentioned as one of the towns where our family operated concrete plants. It was part of the broader West Texas and Eastern New Mexico area where our business expanded. Brownfield was one of many communities connected to our family work.
